title: How Boards and CEOs Can Build a Shared AI Vision
gist: Boards and CEOs agree on the urgency of AI but diverge on realistic speed, scale, and measurable impact; BCG argues boards should coinvest in hands-on AI literacy, secure an unvarnished frontline view of deployments, and learn from cross-industry practitioners to become true governance partners.

insights.claim: Boards and CEOs broadly agree AI deployment is urgent and share the fear of both moving too late and wasting money on unscalable pilots, but they diverge on the speed, scale, and measurable business impact AI can realistically deliver.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Governance friction, not technology, is the gating factor on AI value capture; investors should probe whether a company's board and CEO have a shared, realistic AI ambition rather than assuming urgency equals alignment.
insights.quote: Where boards and CEOs often diverge, however, is over the speed, scale, and measurable business impact that AI realistically can be expected to deliver.
insights.locator: There are few topics on which

insights.claim: For every board that believes its CEO doesn't push the AI agenda decisively enough, there is a CEO who feels their AI ambitions are being slowed down by the board.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: The bottleneck can sit on either side, so diligence should identify which party is the brake rather than assuming management always leads.
insights.quote: for every board that believes its CEO doesn't push the company's AI agenda decisively enough, there's a CEO who feels their AI ambitions are slowed down by the board
insights.locator: Where boards and CEOs often diverge

insights.claim: BCG research shows CEOs are increasingly shaping the AI agenda for their companies, and many feel pressure to show measurable P&L impact from it.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Rising CEO ownership plus P&L-impact pressure signals AI spend is shifting from experimentation toward accountable, near-term return expectations that investors can hold management to.
insights.locator: A certain amount of tension is to be expected

themes.theme: Coinvesting in AI literacy and hands-on experience

insights.claim: Boards should build their own practical AI skillsets alongside CEOs and executives, with many hiring AI coaches and joining personal productivity sessions to reach a baseline literacy needed for proper oversight.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Director-level AI fluency is becoming a governance requirement; boards lacking hands-on experience are a red flag for oversight quality on AI transformation.

insights.claim: A session participant used off-the-shelf tools to build a personal AI 'second brain' that scans her inbox and manages priorities, plus a 'virtual boss' to rehearse tough CEO-style questions and gameplan low-likelihood disruptive scenarios.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that individual directors can reach meaningful AI capability with commodity tools, lowering the bar for expected board-level engagement.
insights.quote: she designed a “virtual boss” that would challenge her with the typical questions and concerns her CEO might raise
insights.locator: At one such session we helped organize

insights.claim: The purpose of joint board-executive AI learning is not to directly shape AI strategy but to build a shared understanding of how fast the technology is moving and the scale of behavioral change AI requires across the organization.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Reframes AI governance as an org-wide change-management challenge rather than a strategy sign-off, which is where transformations most often stall.
insights.quote: it is to gain a shared understanding of how fast the technology is moving—and the degree of behavioral change that AI requires across the entire organization
insights.locator: The purpose of such joint learning opportunities

themes.theme: Frontline visibility and honest capability assessment

insights.claim: Many boards have moved beyond relying solely on CEO or CIO presentations, arranging immersion opportunities to see firsthand where AI is deployed and to learn from frontline employees about their trials and successes.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Ground-truth board access to deployments is a leading indicator that reported AI progress is real rather than a curated 30,000-foot narrative.
insights.locator: Get a Frontline View of AI Deployments

insights.claim: Boards are increasingly pressing management for sober reviews of AI capabilities, demanding to know how impact is being measured, what critical gaps exist including cybersecurity and AI risk, and how the company compares with competitors.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Explicit impact-measurement and competitive-benchmarking demands are the questions investors should expect answered; their absence suggests weak AI governance and unquantified risk.
insights.quote: neither the board nor the executive team can expect all AI initiatives to be successful, but a clear view of the company’s current state is essential for both parties
insights.locator: Increasingly, boards are also pressing management

insights.claim: Boards should seek AI perspectives beyond the usual hyperscalers, tech gurus, and entrepreneurs, tapping their contacts to bring in practitioners across industries who can explain how frontrunners are already creating measurable competitive advantage.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Cross-industry practitioner input is a cheap way to benchmark real deployment outcomes against vendor hype, helping boards separate genuine advantage from marketing.
insights.quote: boards should seek broader perspectives beyond the hyperscalers, tech gurus, and entrepreneurs they typically hear from
insights.locator: Explore the Next Frontier of AI with Practitioners
